feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced water-soluble houseplant fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10), standard strength every 3-4 months or half strength more often

commonIssues: Brown leaf tips from low humidity, dry soil, fertilizer/salt buildup, or fluoride and chlorine in tap water; use distilled or rain water if tips brown.
Root rot from overwatering or poorly draining soil; let the top of the soil dry slightly between thorough waterings.
Over-fertilizing reduces plantlet ('spiderette') formation and can scorch leaf tips.
Direct midday sun can scorch leaves; prefers bright, indirect light.
Occasional pests: spider mites, scale, mealybugs, whiteflies, aphids.

Fleshy tuberous roots fill pots quickly; divide and repot before roots crack the container. Grows and produces plantlets best when slightly pot-bound, so the small recommended pot size is intentional.

source.citation: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center, 'Spider Plant (Chlorophytum comosum)' — bright indirect light, tolerates some direct light (avoid midday sun); allow soil to dry slightly between waterings; day 65-75F (18-24C), night 50-55F (10-13C); fertilize during active growth; excellent hanging baskets; tip burn from salts/fluoride/low humidity; root rot from overwatering.
Clemson HGIC spider plant factsheet — light and temperature guidance (bright indirect light; 65-75F day / 50-55F night ideal).

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder, Chlorophytum comosum — USDA zones 9-11; keep above 45F (~7C) minimum; 55-70F preferred; medium water, keep moderately moist in active growth; avoid over-fertilizing (reduces plantlets); excellent for hanging baskets; tip browning from fluoride/salts; tolerates artificial light.

University of Wisconsin-Madison Extension Horticulture, 'Spider plant, Chlorophytum comosum' — fertilize every 3-4 months at standard strength (or half strength more often); allow soil to dry slightly between thorough waterings; fleshy water-storing roots tolerate inconsistent watering; grows best slightly pot-bound; medium to bright light indoors.
UW-Madison Extension spider plant article — pot-bound preference and rapid root growth used to set small container sizes; trailing plantlet stems make hanging baskets ideal (verticalSuitability).

Confidence note: sources give qualitative guidance ('dry slightly between waterings', 'bright indirect light', no pot volumes). baseIntervalDays=7, targetSunHours=6 (bright indirect, hours of bright light not direct sun), container liters (2-8 L ~ 6-10 inch pot/basket), heatStressC=32, hotWeatherMultiplier=0.75, and seasonality months (plantlets form under fall short days; growth slows in winter) are conservative estimates derived from those qualitative statements, not explicit numbers in the cited pages.
